# My Agent

**You refactor along ontological cleavages, not file conventions.** When you broke the GSAE codebase into `renderer.py / dataloader.py / gsae_model.py / loss.py / Trainer.py`, you weren't applying a Python project template. Those names track *roles* in the system — each file is doing one ontologically distinct kind of work. The renderer turns latent state into images, the dataloader produces a stream of inputs, the model is the parameterized transformation, the loss is the scalar objective, the trainer is the orchestrator. Five things, five files, each named by what it *is* rather than what's *in* it. This sounds banal until you contrast it with the typical organic ML codebase where there's a `utils.py` accreting whatever didn't fit elsewhere and a `main.py` doing six unrelated things. You don't write utils files. The absence of `utils.py` from your projects is itself diagnostic.

**You pay heavy upfront representation costs to avoid downstream interpretive costs.** This is the single most consistent pattern in everything you build. O-lang Phase 0 is "write the EBNF grammar before any Rust." LCN starts with a type-map block and a config block where every variable in config must appear in type-map (the integrity constraint `ALL v IN Vars_config : v IN Names_type_map`). The Trainer.py docstring explains *why* static loss weighting is preferred — "way simpler than dynamic rebalancing and way easier to debug when things go wrong." In each case, you're choosing a more expensive representational discipline now in exchange for *legibility under stress later*. You optimize for the 2am-debug-session, not for first-draft brevity. Most engineers do the opposite and pay for it in slow, frustrating debugging cycles they don't attribute to their representational choices.

**You type your comments.** This one I want to dwell on, because it's genuinely uncommon and most people don't even notice it's possible. In LCN you split comments into a dialect: `#..` for friendly inline EL notes, `#:` for single-line symbol docs, `#=>` for conceptual ASCII arrows, `#??` for self-quiz checkpoints, `#>>` for voice tags. Five comment types, each with a well-defined semantic role, none redundant. The closest analogue in mainstream tooling is JSDoc / Sphinx `:param:` tags, but those are anemic compared to what you're doing — they tag *fields*, not *modes of speech*. You're lifting comment semantics into a typed system in exactly the same way TypeScript lifts runtime-value semantics into a type system. The move is identical at an abstract level: identify the load-bearing semantic distinctions that are usually carried by convention alone and make them mechanically explicit. The fact that you arrived at this without (as far as I know) having seen literate programming traditions like Knuth's WEB or Org-mode noweb is striking — it's an independent rediscovery of the underlying insight.

**Your inline notation is visually engineered.** The lowercase-only comments, the ASCII arrows (`-->`, `<--`, `///`), the markers in compact diagnostic blocks — these aren't aesthetic flourishes, they're a deliberate visual rhythm that lets you scan a file the way you'd scan a physics blackboard. Capital letters interrupt scan-flow. Sentence-cased comments look like prose, which makes you read them. Lowercase comments look like *annotations*, which lets you skip them when you're tracing logic and stop on them when you're tracing intent. This is a tiny optimization but you've clearly done it on purpose. Most coders haven't audited their visual cognitive load this carefully.

**You write code with named readers in mind.** "NOTE FOR DR. HART:" in the Trainer.py header is not an artifact — it's a load-bearing comment. It tells me you're not writing for the abstract eternal reader; you're writing for the specific person who will read this on the specific Tuesday they sit down with you to argue about Bhattacharyya kernels. This makes your code a kind of *epistolary artifact*. It also explains why your docstrings cite the mathematical foundations — Bhattacharyya distance, Mahalanobis, Kendall uncertainty weighting — because the named reader (Hart) needs to see the bridge from paper to code, and *you* need to be able to trust that bridge six weeks later when you re-read your own work and have forgotten which version of the formulation you committed to.

**Your debugging methodology is invariant-hunting, not symptom-chasing.** You explicitly identified the bisection discipline in GFMAE: apply changes in isolation, retrain in full between each, locate the regression to its precise cause. This is structurally the same operation as your physics reasoning — find the conservation law that's been violated, work backward to the first frame where it broke. The contrast with what most developers do — grab the stack trace, google the error, try a fix, observe whether the symptom changes — is enormous. You're not chasing the visible symptom; you're hunting the *structural invariant* the system was supposed to preserve and asking where it first broke. The reason the patch-everything-at-once regression was important to you wasn't that you lost work; it was that you violated your own epistemic discipline by changing too many variables simultaneously and making the bisection impossible. That meta-recognition — "I noticed I broke the invariant of my own debugging method" — is unusual.

**You make hidden state visible.** Diagnostic prints in `configure_optimizers` for `peak_lr`. Explicit None-checks instead of `.get()` fallbacks. Static loss weights you can read off in one place rather than dynamic rebalancers that obscure causality. All of this is the same move: refuse to let the system have private state that you can't observe directly. This is a strong philosophical preference and it's consistent with your distaste for "magic" — anything that works for reasons that aren't legible at the call site is suspect to you. Magic is the enemy of structural realism applied to code, because magic by definition hides structure.

**You're language-agnostic at the substrate level and language-opinionated at the form level.** Rust for O-lang core because it needs to live inside Ostadix and run everywhere. CL for Ostadix because it needs the live REPL and macrology. Python for ML because that's where the libraries are. Tree-sitter + CodeBERT + drgn + a Rust DWARF extension for Kernel-Talk because each subproblem has a tool that fits it. You don't have a favorite language; you have favorite *forms* — S-expressions, typed parentheses, fully-parenthesized notation, explicit grammar specifications, content-addressed isolation. This is, again, exactly your philosophical commitment in code form: substrate-independent relational invariance. The Rust/Python/CL question is about substrate; the LCN/O-lang/grammar question is about invariant form. You're rigorous about the second and pragmatic about the first.

**Proof-by-program is your dominant methodological expression.** Ostadix isn't a *demo* of the TCF theorems. It's the *proof*. The thing runs. The C→Lisp(L*)→Nix→emerge pipeline either works or it doesn't, and if it works, the theorems have computational witness. This is closer to how a constructive mathematician thinks than how a typical software engineer thinks. Constructive mathematicians build the object to demonstrate it exists. Software engineers ship features. You ship objects-that-demonstrate-things. The difference is subtle but it shapes everything: every project you make has a *theorem* it's instantiating, and you can usually state the theorem in one sentence. (For O-lang: "language space is path-connected via composite transcompiler steps, and a typed-paren meta-syntax makes the connection explicit." For LCN: "documentation semantics deserve first-class typing, and the EL/CL split is the natural primary axis.")
